text: this is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English.
zh: 这里是BBC英语教学的《六分钟英语》节目。

text: I'm Phil.
zh: 我是Phil。

text: And I'm Pippa.
zh: 我是Pippa。

text: Are you a big reader, Phil?
zh: Phil,你很爱读书吗?

text: Sometimes yes, sometimes no.
zh: 有时候是,有时候不是。

text: Sometimes I go a while without reading anything.
zh: 有时候我会有一段时间完全不读任何东西。

text: What about you?
zh: 你呢?

text: Well, I really love to read.
zh: 嗯,我真的很喜欢阅读。

text: I read on the train to work all the time.
zh: 我上班坐火车时总是在看书。

text: Oh, well that is a great way to get some reading in, I guess.
zh: 哦,我想那确实是抓紧时间阅读的好办法。

text: Yes, and reading, of course, is a life skill, an essential skill which is useful or important in everyone's life.
zh: 是的,阅读当然是一项生活技能,一种在每个人生活中都有用、都很重要的基本技能。

text: But reading can be a struggle for people with dyslexia, a learning difficulty making it hard for people to read, write or spell.
zh: 但对患有诵读困难症的人来说,阅读可能是一件很吃力的事——那是一种让人难以读、写或拼写的学习障碍。

text: It's thought that around one in ten people in the UK are dyslexic, and many dyslexic children struggle at school where the focus is on reading and writing.
zh: 据估计,英国大约每十人中就有一人患有诵读困难症,许多这样的孩子在以读写为重点的学校里学习吃力。

text: But in this episode, we'll be hearing how dyslexics have other skills which allow them to excel in the workplace.
zh: 但在这一集里,我们将听到诵读困难症患者如何凭借其他技能在职场中脱颖而出。

text: As usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases.
zh: 像往常一样,我们会学习一些实用的新单词和短语。

text: And remember, you'll find all the vocabulary for this episode on our website, bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 别忘了,你可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到这一集的所有词汇。

text: But first, I have a question for you, Phil.
zh: 不过首先,Phil,我有个问题要问你。

text: Studies have shown that people with dyslexia have enhanced abilities in certain areas and can excel in creative thinking, problem solving and inventiveness.
zh: 研究表明,诵读困难症患者在某些方面能力更强,能够在创造性思维、解决问题和创新方面表现出色。

text: So which famous scientist was dyslexic?
zh: 那么,哪位著名科学家患有诵读困难症呢?

text: Was it A, Stephen Hawking, B, Albert Einstein or C, Marie Curie?
zh: 是A,斯蒂芬·霍金;B,阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦;还是C,玛丽·居里?

text: Oh, I think, I think it's B. I think it's Albert Einstein.
zh: 哦,我想是B,我觉得是阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦。

text: Well, we'll find out the answer at the end of the programme.
zh: 好,我们会在节目最后揭晓答案。

text: Now, the British charity Made by Dyslexia shares online learning tools and works with schools and teachers to raise awareness about the condition.
zh: 英国慈善机构Made by Dyslexia在网上分享学习工具,并与学校和教师合作,提高人们对这种病症的认识。

text: The charity recently collaborated with British celebrities who have dyslexia for this promotional video.
zh: 该机构最近与患有诵读困难症的英国名人合作拍摄了这段宣传视频。

text: If you're dyslexic, it's kind of your superpower.
zh: 如果你有诵读困难症,那其实是你的超能力。

text: It's like the way that you think.
zh: 那是你思考问题的方式。

text: Our brains, they're wired to, I think, process information differently.
zh: 我们的大脑天生就以不同的方式处理信息。

text: The way I see the world might be different from somebody else, but that's valid.
zh: 我看待世界的方式可能与别人不同,但这是有价值的。

text: In fact, it's vital.
zh: 事实上,这是至关重要的。

text: You might recognise some of those voices, including actors Orlando Bloom and Keira Knightley and businessman Richard Branson.
zh: 你可能听出了其中一些声音,包括演员奥兰多·布鲁姆和凯拉·奈特莉,以及商人理查德·布兰森。

text: The message is that far from being a disability, dyslexia is their superpower, the ability to do something other people can't.
zh: 这个信息传达的是,诵读困难症远非一种障碍,而是他们的超能力——一种别人做不到的能力。

text: Made by Dyslexia was founded by social entrepreneur Kate Griggs, and Kate co -authored a report showing that empowering dyslexic workers could boost the global economy by billions.
zh: Made by Dyslexia由社会企业家凯特·格里格斯创立,她与人合著了一份报告,指出赋能诵读困难症员工可以为全球经济带来数十亿美元的增长。

text: BBC World Service programme People Fixing the World met Kate at the report's London launch event,
zh: BBC世界服务节目《拯救世界的人们》在该报告的伦敦发布会上采访了凯特。

text: where she explained why being dyslexic can actually give advantages in life, including some exciting job prospects.
zh: 她在会上解释了为什么患有诵读困难症实际上能带来一些人生优势,包括一些令人兴奋的职业前景。

text: If you think about what you have to do as a spy, you have to be reading people really quickly, spotting complex patterns and solving problems really quickly so you can see whether there's a terror threat.
zh: 如果你想想间谍需要做什么——你得能非常迅速地读懂人心,发现复杂的模式,并迅速解决问题,才能判断是否存在恐怖威胁。

text: it's natural that dyslexics are brilliant spies.
zh: 所以诵读困难症患者天生就是出色的间谍。

text: Kate thinks dyslexics make good spies – people who secretly collect information about another country's government or organisations.
zh: 凯特认为诵读困难症患者能成为出色的间谍——那些秘密收集他国政府或组织信息的人。

text: Why?
zh: 为什么呢?

text: Because they're good at reading people.
zh: 因为他们很擅长‘读懂人心’。

text: To read people means to be able to understand someone's true intentions through their body language and behaviour, rather than their words.
zh: “读懂人心”指的是能够通过一个人的肢体语言和行为,而不是言语,来理解其真实意图。

text: Also present at the London charity event was space scientist and dyslexic herself, Dr Maggie Adairin -Pocock.
zh: 同样出席伦敦慈善活动的还有太空科学家、同样患有诵读困难症的玛吉·艾德林-波洛克博士。

text: Here, Maggie tells BBC World Service's People Fixing the World how being dyslexic contributes different skills and ideas to her project teams.
zh: 在这里,玛吉向BBC世界服务的《拯救世界的人们》讲述了诵读困难症如何为她的项目团队带来不同的技能和想法。

text: Well, I think it's recognising people's skills and differences.
zh: 嗯,我认为这关乎认识到人们的技能和差异。

text: It's about teamwork and collaboration.
zh: 这关乎团队合作与协作。

text: We do it in science and that's when we reach for the stars.
zh: 我们在科学领域正是这样做的,这样我们才能‘摘星揽月’。

text: But to do that we need to know our skills and value all the skills.
zh: 但要做到这一点,我们需要了解自己的技能,并重视所有的技能。

text: Maggie thinks that everyone's skills should be valued or considered important.
zh: 玛吉认为每个人的技能都应该被重视,或者说被认为是重要的。

text: Dyslexics may struggle to read or spell, but their skills in problem solving and communication, for example, can be essential for scientific success.
zh: 诵读困难症患者可能在阅读或拼写上有困难,但他们在解决问题和沟通方面的技能,例如,对科学上的成功可能至关重要。

text: By valuing everyone's contribution, business and science teams can reach for the stars,
zh: 通过重视每个人的贡献,商业和科学团队才能‘摘星揽月’,

text: an idiom meaning to be ambitious and try to achieve something difficult even if it seems impossible.
zh: 这个习语的意思是有雄心壮志,即使看似不可能,也要努力实现一件困难的事情。

text: And talking of reaching for the stars, Pippa, I think it's time you revealed the answer to your question about successful scientists.
zh: 说到‘摘星揽月’,Pippa,我想是时候揭晓你那个关于成功科学家的问题的答案了。

text: Yes, I asked you which famous scientist was dyslexic.
zh: 是的,我问你哪位著名科学家患有诵读困难症。

text: Was it…
a Stephen Hawking, b Albert Einstein or c Marie Curie and you were right Phil, it was Albert Einstein.
zh: A,斯蒂芬·霍金;B,阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦;还是C,玛丽·居里——Phil,你答对了,是阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦。

text: OK, let's recap the vocabulary we've learned, starting with life skill, an essential skill such as reading or problem solving which is useful in everyone's life.
zh: 好,我们来回顾一下今天学到的词汇,首先是life skill(生活技能),指像阅读或解决问题这样,对每个人生活都有用的基本技能。

text: A superpower is the ability to do something that other people can't.
zh: superpower(超能力)指做别人做不到的事情的能力。

text: A spy is someone who secretly collects information about other countries' governments or organisations.
zh: spy(间谍)指秘密收集他国政府或组织信息的人。

text: If you're good at reading people, you can understand people's real intentions through how they act and behave instead of what they say.
zh: 如果你善于‘读懂人心’(good at reading people),你就能通过人们的行为举止,而不是言语,理解他们真实的意图。

text: If you value something or someone, you consider them to be important.
zh: 如果你‘重视’(value)某事或某人,就是认为它或他很重要。

text: And finally, the idiom to reach for the stars means to have high or ambitious aims and try to achieve something difficult, even if it seems impossible.
zh: 最后,习语‘reach for the stars’(摘星揽月)的意思是拥有远大或雄心勃勃的目标,并努力实现一件困难的事情,即使它看似不可能。

text: Once again, our six minutes are up, but remember you can find many more trending topics, plus a quiz and worksheet for this episode, on our website bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 我们的六分钟又到时间了,别忘了你可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到更多热门话题,以及本集的测验和练习题。

text: Hope to see you there soon, but for now it's goodbye.
zh: 希望很快在那里见到你,不过现在,再见。

text: Goodbye.
zh: 再见。
